column1: Twin/Brother Narratives Across Time and Cultures
Shared characteristics: youth, horses, rescue, dawn/sun associations
Developed in Vedic, Avestan, Greek, and Roman traditions
Research Note: Thomas as the Divine Twin of Jesus — Structural Analysis

The Gospel of Thomas opens: 'These are the secret sayings which the living Jesus spoke and which Didymos Judas Thomas wrote down.' Didymos (Greek) and Thomas (Aramaic) both mean 'twin.' The text never specifies whose twin Thomas is, but the early Syriac Acts of Thomas (c. 200–225 CE) explicitly calls Thomas the twin brother of Jesus, and the two are at points visually indistinguishable.

Structural position in the divine twins motif: In the comparative table, Jesus-Thomas maps onto the PIE twin archetype as follows: one twin is 'divine' (Jesus, the cosmic savior), the other 'mortal' or 'doubting' (Thomas, who demands physical proof of resurrection). This mirrors the Dioscuri (Castor mortal / Pollux divine), Osiris/Set (divine order / chaos), and even Apollo/Artemis (light/darkness, celestial/terrestrial). The pattern of one twin dying and the other surviving — or one receiving divine initiation and the other being excluded — recurs across the PIE corpus.

Gnostic development: In the Gospel of Thomas, Thomas is not a doubter but a privileged recipient of secret teachings unavailable to the other disciples. This inverts the canonical New Testament role entirely. In Gnostic framing, Thomas represents the 'inner twin' or spiritual double of Jesus — the awakened human consciousness that mirrors the divine. This is structurally analogous to Mithras-Sol in the Roman mysteries, where the two figures are cosmically paired but distinct in function.

The Bodhisattva parallel: Some scholars (including Guy Stroumsa) have noted that the Thomas-Jesus twin tradition may have developed in Edessa/Syria under influence from Buddhist missionaries via the Silk Road, with the 'spiritual twin' concept paralleling the Buddhist notion of the practitioner awakening their own inner Buddha-nature (buddha-dhatu) — the cosmic teacher who is simultaneously other and self.

Tier classification: Thomas = twin (etymological fact, Tier A). Thomas as Jesus's literal twin brother in Syriac tradition (Tier A for that tradition, not canonical). Thomas as structural divine-twin parallel in the PIE motif scheme (Tier B). Thomas-Buddha transmission via Edessa (Tier C, speculative).
